subject: feat(provider): remove gemini as a shipped provider
decision: gemini is no longer a shipped provider.
why: the CLI is deprecated and its backend returns 410 Gone since

decision: SPINE: Ralph is a supervised-execution runtime. The open-ended 'configure a CLI however' provider model was a wrong turn and is replaced.
why: User reframing. Non-negotiable control invariant: an underlying agent CLI must NEVER block the system (no permission prompts, no clarification waits, no interactive menus). Ralph owns each agent's stdio/pty, runs every agent NON-INTERACTIVELY only (--print/exec/run, never interactive modes), and watches each stream. Substrate: Bubble Tea (already in repo); tea.ExecProcess handles terminal restore/exec/suspend natively in the Elm update loop; organized tmux-like live panes, one per agent. Watchdogs: no-output-for-N (stall), underlying agent PID health, stream-content prompt/stall signals. On a would-be block: auto-answer | deny | kill+reclaim, NEVER wait. Kill is cheap because context is durable: point-in-time restoration = replay the killed worker's plan-scoped state to a fresh worker. This ties the control invariant to the durability/reaper story: the plan DB is the source of truth for 'what each worker is doing', scoped+durable enough to replay a killed task.

decision: The TUI process IS the long-running runtime. Collapse the durable-service/daemon apparatus.
why: User: committing to our own foreground TUI means it IS long-running for as long as the work needs. This eliminates internal/service (launchd/systemd/windows service install), the detached durable daemon, and much of the socket IPC whose sole purpose was a TUI attaching to a background service. The TUI and executor become ONE process. Durability across a TUI crash is the plan DB + point-in-time restoration (relaunch -> replay state), NOT a background daemon. Consequence: large parts of the earlier review's findings about the durable service, socket lifecycle, service-manager integration, and daemon-reaper become moot or reshaped. The reaper survives but as an in-process sweep for workers killed WITHIN a live TUI session, plus a startup reconcile that replays orphaned in-progress tasks on relaunch.

decision: Attach model = stream/subscribe (docker-compose + tmux), NOT live pty handoff. Agent-decided from research.
why: Full-autonomy mandate: this is an implementation-architecture call to make from research, not defer to user. Dissected docker-compose: the DAEMON owns containers+their stdio; 'compose up' is a CLIENT that attaches by copying the output stream in a goroutine; 'up -d' detaches the client while the daemon keeps running; reattach = a fresh client subscribing to the daemon's stream; Ctrl-P Ctrl-Q detaches the viewer without killing work because viewer != work-owner. Ptys NEVER move between processes. tmux server/client is the same shape. DECISION: the engine that owns the agent ptys publishes a structured output+event stream persisted to the plan DB (scrollback) + a live channel; every front-end (Bubble Tea TUI, radioactive_ralph logs, CI assertion harness) is a SUBSCRIBER that replays recent scrollback + tails live. No SCM_RIGHTS fd-passing in the design (kept as a documented future escape hatch only). This kills the fragile 'shift live processes to foreground' idea and replaces it with the proven compose/tmux pattern.

decision: Serviceless-headless backgrounding = re-exec pattern (parent exits, child setsid/Setpgid resumes from DB), NOT fork/daemonize.
why: Research: Go cannot fork() (kills all goroutines), so true daemonize is unsafe. The industry pattern (go-daemon, gohup, HAProxy-style) is re-exec: respawn self with a detach flag, parent exits, child gets its own session (setsid / Setpgid) and resumes work from the plan DB. Consistent with the durability model (a detached run is a fresh process replaying DB state, not a literally-backgrounded current process). Fallback when even re-exec is undesirable: run blocking and suggest nohup/screen/tmux.

decision: Use REAL tmux as the durability+transport substrate via gotmux; the tmux server is the long-running process. Deletes daemon/re-exec/socket-attach/SCM_RIGHTS.
why: User proposal + agent research. gotmux (MIT, typed, pkg.go.dev, shells to tmux binary) wraps sessions/windows/panes/SendKeys/Attach/ListSessions and exposes all tmux fields (incl pane_dead); anything unwrapped (pipe-pane) we call via the tmux binary directly. tmux server already IS the docker-compose-daemon/tmux model, battle-tested 20 years, not our code: owns every pane pty, keeps panes alive after client detach, any number of attach/detach, survives client death, scrollback. So: Ralph creates a session, runs each agent in a PANE (window per squad/role, pane per worker), can exit while the session keeps running; attach=tmux attach, headless=detached session, 'radioactive_ralph logs'=pane capture. This DELETES the durable daemon, the re-exec backgrounding, socket-attach, and SCM_RIGHTS from the design. Windows: WSL or psmux (github.com/psmux/psmux) suggested for native. A supervisor 'service' becomes OPTIONAL (auto-restart the orchestrator only) since tmux+plan-DB already provide durability.

decision: Hybrid agent I/O: structured result via file/fd (clean data), pane via pipe-pane (human stream + watchdog). Never scrape data from rendered terminal.
why: Capturing a rendered tmux pane is lossy (wrapping/ANSI/redraw), but our spend/usage/result parsing needs clean JSON (claude --print, opencode run --format json). So each agent pane runs the CLI non-interactively with its structured result written to a file/fd Ralph reads DIRECTLY for data, while the pane is the human-visible stream captured via tmux pipe-pane -> fifo for the watchdog (stall/prompt detection) + DB scrollback. Pane = observation+control (send-keys, kill-pane, pane_dead); file = data. The 'never block' control invariant stays OUR logic (watchdog on piped stream + pane_dead + no-output timeout); tmux provides only the plumbing.

decision: CORRECTION: the tmux/gotmux substrate is the LEADING CANDIDATE, not final. A substrate eval (3mux/sunder/bubbletea+creack-pty/gotmux) is running to decide.
why: User named 3mux, sunder, and 'Bubble Tea+lipgloss could multiplex natively' as worth studying before locking tmux. Correct process: don't commit the substrate on the first plausible option. Cloned all to ~/src/reference-codebases and dispatched a 4-way code-study workflow scoring each against the control invariant (own pty + read stream programmatically), durability (survive front-end death), streaming, Windows portability, deps. Option D (own ptys via creack/pty for control + Bubble Tea for rendering + tmux OPTIONAL for detach durability) is explicitly on the table and may win over pure-tmux. The earlier 'use real tmux via gotmux' decision stands only as the leading candidate pending this eval.

decision: SUBSTRATE DECIDED (reverses the tmux lean): Ralph owns agent ptys directly via creack/pty; Bubble Tea renders; NO tmux/gotmux. 4-way code-study workflow.
why: Code-study of 3mux/sunder/bubbletea/gotmux against our requirements. tmux FAILS the control invariant: the tmux server (not Ralph) forks the agent and holds the pty, so every read/write/kill is an os/exec round-trip to a process Ralph doesn't own; pipe-pane isn't wrapped by gotmux, capture-pane is snapshot-polling not a stream, so you hand-build the tee/watchdog anyway ON TOP OF an extra failure domain (external binary, socket reachability, unverified native-Windows psmux). 3mux (real pty ownership but package-main, Unix-only SCM_RIGHTS detach, single-maintainer, not importable) and sunder (dead, single-process, can't kill) both rejected. WINNER: creack/pty (MIT, de-facto-standard Go pty lib, what 3mux forked from and sunder vendors, real ConPTY Windows support). Ralph calls pty.Start(cmd), holds *os.File + cmd.Process per agent: direct Write() input, Process.Kill() instant kill, tee the ptmx reader (branch->Bubble Tea pane, branch->watchdog), structured results via a JSON fd/file Ralph passes to the CLI (never scrape the rendered pane). All Go channels/goroutines, no fifo indirection. DURABILITY without tmux: keep the EXISTING supervisor/client split (internal/service owns ptys + re-execs headless via os.StartProcess detached; internal/ipc socket; tui is a thin restartable client that only renders) — the tmux client/server SHAPE without tmux's process or Windows gap. This VINDICATES internal/service + internal/ipc (do NOT delete them) and corrects the earlier 'TUI-as-runtime, delete the daemon' overreach. STRONGEST RISK: a supervisor crash takes all ptys down (no external tmux keeping them alive); mitigate by keeping the supervisor minimal+boring, OS-service-managed restart (launchd/systemd), and making supervisor/IPC the top test target (TUI-crash-reattach, supervisor-restart-reclaim). Full report: scratchpad/substrate-eval.md.

decision: FINAL ARCHITECTURE SHAPE: one binary radioactive_ralph with --supervisor; supervisor owns everything (ptys via creack/pty + headless work) and holds it open; client is a read-only live Bubble Tea view. No interaction with agents. Attach/detach nomenclature dropped.
why: User simplification. Nobody INTERACTS with agents — goal is read-only observation + block/stall/no-output detection + resource watchdog. So: (1) ONE binary radioactive_ralph, --supervisor flag; supervisor is distributed via the Go service framework OR run under nohup/anything; it is THE process that holds open both headless work and every agent binary, owning each pty (creack/pty) 'in its own terminal' — the DEVELOPER never needs that terminal, radioactive_ralph needs it as the CONTROL LAYER. (2) The client REFUSES to run without a supervisor instance somewhere on the system -> need a cross-platform way to ADVERTISE a listening supervisor; single-supervisor (refuse a 2nd if one detected). (3) Agent output -> rotating/compressed logs (structlog-style), NOT held in memory -> flat memory over days; sessions are durable but not one-giant-run (different Ralphs/roles at different times), so memory doesn't spike from role rotation either. (4) Resource watchdog: watch agent procs for mem/CPU beyond CONFIGURABLE thresholds, recommend safe defaults from system config, stored in a USER-level XDG config dir; a USER-level DB (XDG) holds process tracking etc. XDG libs cover Win/Mac/Linux. (5) 'attach/detach' is the WRONG term — it's seamless: run the client (no --supervisor) and the Bubble Tea/Lipgloss TUI organizes panes at MACRO/MESO/MICRO: top = project plan + overall hierarchy; drill the plan to talk to the PM/team-lead; drill the hierarchy to squads -> singular -> one agent's log tail. This keeps creack/pty as the control layer and the supervisor/client split, but reframes it as supervisor-holds-everything + client-is-live-view. Confirms internal/service (distribution) survives; the socket/advertisement layer survives but as supervisor-discovery, not attach.

decision: Supervisor discovery = the socket IS the advertisement at a well-known XDG runtime path; reuse existing internal/ipc + PID-lock + heartbeat.
why: Research + repo already has 80%. Supervisor binds unix-socket(Mac/Linux)/named-pipe(Windows) at an XDG runtime path (internal/ipc does this). Client discovery=try connect (success=live, fail=no supervisor->refuse/offer-start). Single-instance: bind IS the mutex + PID lockfile (flock.go acquirePIDLock exists) detects a stale socket from a crashed supervisor (dead PID->reclaim). Heartbeat file confirms liveness. Repurpose existing IPC+PID+heartbeat from 'attach to durable service' into 'discover the supervisor'.

decision: Client is DUMB (talk to supervisor + init config only). --supervisor shares the SAME init logic but for USER-level config. Coordination protocol + config model designed together on ONE branch.
why: User. The client exists only to (a) talk to the supervisor and (b) initialize config. --supervisor mode reuses the SAME initialization logic (wizard TUI | flags | existing-config-by-path) but at USER level. Because how things coordinate impacts the protocol, and client/server comms + config location are interdependent, the whole thing is designed on ONE large branch with ONE comprehensive directive set, not split specs.

decision: NO per-project SQLite DBs. ONE user-level SQLite DB (XDG) is durable memory for ALL projects (plan state, process tracking, per-project config). Repos stay CLEAN — zero committed dirs by default.
why: User. Because --supervisor always runs first and always, it can own ONE user-level XDG SQLite DB as durable memory across all projects: plan DAG state, process tracking, and per-project CONFIG (stored IN the DB, NOT as repo files, and explicitly NOT like the current .agent-state skill pattern). Supervisor makes regular backups of its own DB. Nothing is committed to a repo by default -> no collisions, no merge issues, no repo litter — radioactive-ralph is CLEAN. A user MAY opt in by pointing config/DB at an in-repo path or git-tracking their user config (document this), but it's never the default. This eliminates the plandag-per-repo + .radioactive-ralph/ committed-dir model.

decision: No separate optional per-project config concept. Project config lives in the user DB. --init explicitly (re)initializes; plain run in an unknown dir auto-routes to init.
why: User. radioactive_ralph --init explicitly initializes or re-initializes a project. Plain 'radioactive_ralph' in a directory: if that directory is not yet a known project in the USER sqlite db, auto-route to init; otherwise run. --supervisor makes the directory irrelevant (uses XDG). There is no 'optional project config file' as a first-class concept — project configuration is DB-resident.

decision: Project identity = accumulated fingerprints, not fragile absolute paths. Git heuristics for git dirs; absolute path seed for non-git; identifiers ACCUMULATE across git-init and moves.
why: User. Absolute-path identity is fragile (moves/renames break it). Instead fingerprint a project: for a git directory use git heuristics (e.g. first-commit sha / remote / root markers); for a non-git directory seed with the absolute path as an identifier. Identifiers ACCUMULATE: a non-git dir later git-initialized transparently gains its git identifier(s) ON TOP of the path identifier, so the same project stays recognized across the git transition and across directory moves. Stored in the user DB.

decision: Config is a virtual merge layer: user+project config merged; an explicitly-passed config (either mode) overrides on top; validate the MERGED layer and exit-on-error reporting exactly what's missing.
why: User. Both --supervisor (user-level) and regular (project-level) mode accept config via headless FLAGS, a TUI WIZARD, or a config PASSED BY PATH — all feeding one pipeline: build a virtual config by merging loaded user + project config, then layer a passed-by-path config on TOP as highest-precedence OVERRIDES. Validation runs against the MERGED virtual layer: if required pieces are missing after merge, exit with an error that reports exactly what must be defined. A documented path exists to author a project-level config file, but it is an OVERRIDE SOURCE, not a required committed artifact. One validation mechanism regardless of source.

decision: Three override config-file flags + two virtual layers + change-vs-override semantics with supervisor conflict-diff warning.
why: User, closing the config-resolution gaps. THREE flags (all override sources): --config-file/-C (joint config, may have a projects: stanza), --user-config-file (user-specific, may ALSO have projects:, same loader), --project-config-file (one project; IGNORED in --supervisor mode). TWO virtual layers built in order: (1) virtual USER config = DB config < --config-file < --user-config-file; (2) virtual PROJECTS config (in the supervisor, per project) = all-projects-from-DB < projects:-stanza-from-virtual-USER-config. CHANGE vs OVERRIDE: the CLIENT signals its heuristics (fingerprints) + any project-config CHANGES to the supervisor. CHANGES occur via headless/TUI wizard OR explicit --init (new/redone init); in that mode a --project-config-file is treated as CHANGES -> merged on top of the virtual user.projects for that project AND stored to the DB. In NORMAL client mode (non-init), a --project-config-file signals OVERRIDES not CHANGES -> project keeps its stored init unmodified; the file merges on top of virtual user.projects for that project at RUNTIME ONLY (not persisted). SUPERVISOR conflict warning: if project config from --config-file or --user-config-file's projects: stanza would OVERRIDE a stored project's settings, supervisor does backwards-looking diffing and warns explicitly that the user must either keep passing that config as --project-config-file OR remove the conflicts — and OFFERS to remove them automatically (trivial once computed).

decision: Migration = clean-slate rewrite on the feat/supervisor-architecture branch (old model removed early, new model built wholesale), few/one large PR at the end.
why: User choice. Nothing is in production and the redesign overrides the core model (per-repo plandag, committed config dir, attach-daemon, open-ended providers), so maintaining a strangler around code we're deleting is wasted work. The branch WILL be incoherent mid-flight — acceptable since it is not main. Implementation implication I will bake into the plan: sequence as 'build each subsystem to a build/test-GREEN checkpoint in isolation, then integrate' (NOT 'delete everything then rebuild blind'), so regressions are catchable at each internal checkpoint rather than only at the end. The final PR is large but reviewable as 'here is the new architecture'. Review findings (reaper->supervisor reclaim, CI-gating, dispatch test gaps, doc drift, dead rlog, turn_timeout) fold into the rewrite at their natural points rather than as separate PRs against the old model.

decision: VARIANTS DIE ENTIRELY. radioactive_ralph is ONE persisting, flexible, mutating Ralph that twists into whatever the plan needs. Prompts are 'you're an agent, here's the task+context', NOT persona roleplay.
why: User. The 10 variants (blue/green/savage/...) go away. There is one mutating Ralph; parallelism and posture are JUDGMENT CALLS (headless or TUI) decomposed by plan structure, not baked personas. System/user prompts become minimal: 'you are an agent, here is what you are doing, here is the necessary context, you are intelligent enough to figure it out' — NOT 'you are an expert NodeJS developer'. This massively simplifies prompting. The earlier Fixit<->Professor pairing survives as STAGES of the planning genesis, not as variants. This overrides U3a variant-audit-produces-a-lineup and the whole variant/registry/profile system.

decision: Plans are SIMPLE MARKDOWN decomposed HEURISTICALLY over the AST (goldmark) — no LLM, no structured output, no vectors. Heading=nesting group; unordered list=parallel steps; ordered list=sequential steps; don't descend past a heading that has subheadings.
why: User. A markdown format so simple no agent can break it: every heading level indicates a nesting group; under a heading anything goes (bullets, prose, one long sentence). Dependency ordering = heading ORDER (# first ... # next = sequential groups) + list TYPE under a subheading (unordered=parallelizable steps, ordered=sequential steps; bullets+paragraphs=one step with detail). The orchestrator parses with goldmark into an AST and computes past/present/future purely heuristically: what is pending at heading level N? subheadings vs wall-of-text vs bullets vs ordered? Don't descend past a level-N heading that has subheadings (implies dependency ordering). NO LLM and NO structured-output needed for decomposition. Vectors/RAG for plans judged brittle; goldmark+validated-simple-format+AST is the leading path (pending lib eval). Optionally a PLAN CONFIG (virtual-merge, dot-notation coords like s1.1: parallel:false, step_delimiter) assists translating user intent — fleshed out later.

decision: Planning genesis: agents JUXTAPOSE/CHALLENGE to refine a prompt into a full end-to-end plan. Headless->final markdown doc; TUI->read that doc on screen (scroll + embedded/$EDITOR editing); allow skipping planning. We SIDESTEP 'extract questions' — the refined markdown IS the surface.
why: User. The one vaguely-interactive moment is planning genesis, but we never talk TO an agent at the developer level. Instead a TEAM of agents juxtapose and challenge each other to refine the initial input (inline prompt OR full markdown) until it covers everything beginning-to-end. HEADLESS mode: produce a final markdown plan document. TUI mode: READ that markdown to the screen (arrow/page-up-down scroll), with an embedded editor OR open-in-$EDITOR (both options). Users may also SKIP the planning stage and use input as-is. This circumvents 'how do we extract questions' entirely — the refined document is the review surface. Fixit<->Professor (or whichever) run as STAGES of this refinement.

decision: Completion is ORCHESTRATOR-VERIFIED, never agent-asserted. A step is not done because an agent says so or TERMINATES (termination may be an error). A top-most Ralph (team-lead or alongside) verifies against the plan.
why: User. We cannot trust an agent's self-report of done, and ESPECIALLY not termination (could be a crash/error). By necessity another Ralph runs at the top-most layer (the team-lead itself, or alongside it) and verifies each step's completion against the plan before marking it done in the DB. This is the durability+correctness backbone.

decision: Context/liveness: don't detect auto-compaction; send periodic ENFORCEMENT prompts (stay on task; fan out if able; self-check). On MANUAL end -> kill+restart fresh. Each agent gets an XDG decision-log markdown, later ABSORBED by the team-lead into history.
why: User. Avoid the hard problem of detecting when an agent auto-compresses context. Instead periodically send enforcement prompts ensuring the agent stays on task (and if it can spawn its own subagents/workflows, do so; else self-check at the next convenient point). When an agent MANUALLY hits its end, kill and start fresh. Each agent writes its own decision-log markdown file in XDG; the team-lead ABSORBS those into project history to determine next steps. (A2A may apply here too — see lib eval.)

decision: Agent-CLI capability profiles must record native subagent/workflow/parallelism support (some LLMs fan out themselves).
why: User. The per-CLI profile must record whether the underlying API/CLI has native subagents, workflows, or parallelism — because some agents can themselves fan out, which the orchestrator should exploit (delegate a parallel group to a fan-out-capable agent rather than spawning N Ralph-managed agents). This is a capability flag on the provider binding, distinct from the (now-dead) variant personas.

decision: sqlite-memory and sqlite-sync are OUT: both are C SQLite loadable extensions, incompatible with our pure-Go modernc.org/sqlite (no C extension ABI). Plans use goldmark + validated markdown, no vectors.
why: Verified directly: both repos bundle sqlite3.c/sqlite3.h and are classic C loadable extensions (sqlite3_extension_init shape). modernc.org/sqlite is a pure-Go reimplementation with NO C-extension loading, so neither can load without abandoning modernc for CGO mattn/go-sqlite3 (losing pure-Go cross-compile + no-CGO builds). Hard no for our stack. This vindicates the goldmark + validated-simple-markdown-format + heuristic-AST-decomposition path and confirms vectors/RAG for plans are out (brittle AND incompatible). a2abridge is a Go module with cmd/ + internal/ — likely usable as a service/binary but its internal/ blocks library import; A2A comms adoption decided by the running lib-eval workflow.

decision: goldmark (MIT, pure-Go) CONFIRMED as the plan-AST parser: exposes Heading.Level (nesting groups) and List.IsOrdered() (sequential vs parallel) + walkable AST. Heuristic decomposition is feasible.
why: Verified directly in the clone: ast/block.go has type Heading{Level int} and List.IsOrdered(). So the pure-heuristic 'no LLM' decomposition (heading level = group, ordered list = sequential steps, unordered = parallel steps, don't descend past a heading with subheadings) maps 1:1 onto the goldmark AST. MIT license, Go 1.22, pure-Go (no CGO) — compatible with our stack. This is the load-bearing novel piece and it holds; ADOPT goldmark.

decision: ADOPT the official a2aproject/a2a-go SDK as the A2A vocabulary (import the a2a/ core-types pkg). Keep durability, pty control, and orchestrator-verified completion OURS. REJECT a2abridge.
why: User pointed to a2aproject/a2a-go (the OFFICIAL Apache-2.0 A2A SDK), which reverses the lib-eval's 'build our own types' conclusion (that evaluated the third-party a2abridge, not the official SDK). Verified: a2a-go v2 exposes IMPORTABLE packages (a2a/, a2aclient/, a2asrv/, a2apb/, a2agrpc/) — NOT stuck under internal/ like a2abridge. The a2a/ core-types package (Task, TaskState, Message, AgentCard) imports ONLY stdlib (encoding/gob, encoding/json, fmt) + its own errordetails — NO grpc/protobuf/http/CGO — so we can import the A2A vocabulary cleanly and pure-Go, pulling in the heavier a2asrv/a2agrpc/a2apb only if we ever want a real network-facing A2A surface. TaskState is a PLAIN string enum (TaskStateSubmitted/Working/Completed/Failed/InputRequired/...); WE drive transitions, so orchestrator-verified completion maps cleanly — a2abridge's fatal 'agent self-completes' flaw was its MCP TOOL design, not the A2A protocol. TaskStateInputRequired is exactly the never-block signal (needing input = a task state the orchestrator handles, not a blocked pty). a2asrv is INTERFACES we implement (AgentExecutor, AgentCardProducer), not a forced server. DECISION: adopt a2a-go for the standardized vocabulary + optional server-interface surface; keep the SQLite durability, the creack/pty control layer, and orchestrator-owned completion transitions as ours. Standards-aligned for free, could expose a real A2A edge later. REJECT a2abridge (internal/-only, inverted completion, no durability) and sqlite-memory/sqlite-sync (C extensions, incompatible with modernc).

decision: Use cobra + viper (standard) for CLI + config, replacing kong. No binary-size constraint; heavier deps are fine.
why: User: there is no size requirement; cobra/viper are standard and should be part of it. cobra is ALREADY in our dep tree via a2a-go, so consolidating the CLI on cobra (replacing the current kong) removes a redundant framework and aligns with the ecosystem. viper is a strong fit for the virtual-merge config model (§5a): it natively merges defaults < config-file < env < flags with precedence and supports multiple config files, directly supporting the USER/PROJECTS layering rather than hand-rolling all merge logic (we still own the change-vs-override semantics, the projects: stanza layering, and the conflict-diff, but viper does the mechanical file/env/flag merge underneath). CLARIFICATION of scope: the earlier 'avoid heavy deps / pure-Go' reasoning applies ONLY to the SQLite engine (modernc pure-Go vs CGO mattn is a build-COMPATIBILITY issue: C extensions can't load into modernc, and CGO loses no-CGO cross-compile) — it was NOT a binary-size rule and does not restrict cobra/viper/grpc/etc.

subject: fix(cassette): data race on recorder start time (#70)

decision: Added [WAIT-AGENT] as a first-class directive wait-state tag (global anti-stop hook). Use it for items delegated to a running background executor.
why: User granted ownership to expand the global agent-state hook system. Problem: when the orchestrator delegates a directive item to a background Workflow/subagent executor (which re-invokes the session on completion), the item is genuinely in-flight but the hook only documented [WAIT]/[WAIT-REVIEW] (dead-end external waits). The regex WAIT-[A-Z]+ already MATCHED WAIT-AGENT, so functionally it worked, but it wasn't documented/first-class. Fix (backed up ~/.claude/hooks first per CLAUDE.md): documented [WAIT-AGENT] in the hook's comment + emitted help text as 'delegated to a running background executor (re-invokes on completion) — a legitimate active yield, not a dead-end external wait'. No regex change needed. Convention: mark a directive item [WAIT-AGENT] while its executor runs; flip to [x] after verifying the checkpoint on return.

decision: TUI/E2E testing is TWO layers: teatest for the model, + a real-binary pty expect/send E2E that drives the actual binary like a user (supervisor + --init + TUI keystrokes).
why: User: screenshots prove nothing; we need to actually RUN the TUI like a user, in supervisor AND project-init flows. Research (charmbracelet teatest, creack/pty expect/send, atago, testscript): Layer 1 = github.com/charmbracelet/x/exp/teatest — drive the tea.Model directly with tea.KeyMsg, assert output/golden/state (fast, deterministic, CI-friendly; tests macro->meso->micro drill logic in isolation, no real supervisor). Layer 2 = REAL-BINARY E2E: build the radioactive_ralph binary, spawn it under a pty via creack/pty (already vendored — no new dep), and drive an EXPECT/SEND loop: start --supervisor in the background, run --init in a temp project dir, launch the client TUI, send real keystrokes (arrows/enter/esc/q), assert on the rendered terminal output. This is the 'like an actual user' test. A small in-repo expect helper (write to ptmx, read+scan for expected substrings with a timeout) suffices — no heavy expect dep. Fixtures: seed the temp project from ~/src/reference-codebases/test-repo. Split: teatest + fake-provider dispatch = CI-feasible (no real agent spend); the real-binary pty E2E with REAL claude/codex/opencode under a spend cap = local-developer-only (env-gated). Phase 8 builds both.

decision: creack/pty is Unix-only (returns ErrUnsupported on Windows — no real ConPTY). Native-Windows pty agent execution is NOT supported; Windows users use WSL. Guard/skip pty-dependent tests on Windows.
why: CI (windows-latest) proved it: internal/agent tests fail 'Start: unsupported' because creack/pty's start_windows.go is a stub, not a ConPTY impl — the earlier design claim of 'real ConPTY Windows support' was WRONG. The supervised-execution spine (Ralph owns the agent pty) therefore does not run natively on Windows. RESOLUTION: (1) the pty-owned agent runtime is documented as Unix (macOS/Linux) + WSL for Windows — matches the existing 'WSL or psmux' Windows guidance; a native-Windows ConPTY agent runner is a future task, not this rewrite; (2) build-guard / t.Skip the pty-dependent tests (internal/agent, and the sh-command tests which also need a POSIX shell) on Windows so CI is green; (3) also fix two unrelated Windows test-portability bugs found: internal/service's launchd plist test must be build-tagged off Windows (it's a macOS artifact), and internal/supervisor's stale-socket-reclaim test writes a file placeholder that doesn't work for Windows named pipes (guard or adapt).

decision: Close the 6 P1 review gaps on PR #73: the runtime had no real end-to-end user path. (1) plan-creation CLI: add 'radioactive_ralph plan import <file>' + 'plan ls' client subcommands calling store.CreatePlan/SetPlanStatus(active) so a user can actually seed a plan; (2) dispatch driver: supervisor's periodic tick now also calls DispatchNext for every active plan (bounded), not only the reaper — active plans progress on their own without a test-only Enqueue caller; (3) worker WorkingDir: thread the resolved project checkout (project abs_path fingerprint) into provider.Request.WorkingDir and orch.projectDirFor, instead of '.' (supervisor cwd is irrelevant); add store.ProjectAbsPath accessor; (4) real acceptance: derive Acceptance from plan markdown fenced 'accept:' annotations (command/file_exists) so VerifyAndComplete re-runs a real check instead of accepting any non-empty output; (5) config-backed binding resolver: runSupervisorMode builds orch with a BindingResolver that reads stored vconfig (default_provider/provider keys) via provider.File, so stored config selects the provider instead of always claude; (6) SilenceErrors:true on root cmd so cobra doesn't double-print the error run() already formats.
why: CodeRabbit/reviewer P1s on PR #73, all confirmed real by reading the code (verify.go/orchestrator.go/supervisor.go admit the gaps in their own comments: 'not implemented in this phase', 'always returns .', 'no non-test caller'). User standing directive: 'never a better time to implement missing features than now — leaving tech debt for later hides things.' These are the exact tech debt the reviewer surfaced; deferring them would ship a runtime that can't be driven end-to-end by a real user and whose verification gate accepts a worker's excuse as success.

decision: Fix the Windows 'Temp project + supervisor smoke' CI step: rename its PowerShell $home variable to $homeDir. $HOME is a read-only PowerShell automatic variable, so 'let $home = Join-Path ...' threw WriteError at script line 19 and killed the step before any Ralph assertion ran.
why: windows-latest Test job's go test ./... passed (all the pty/socket/service portability fixes worked), but this separate pwsh smoke step failed with a bare WriteError. Root cause was a reserved-automatic-variable collision in the workflow I authored (commit f5d1912, Phase 8) — not a Ralph bug. The step never actually exercised the 'client fails without supervisor' assertion it claims to; the rename lets it run for real.
